skill.name: golang-performance
skill.description: Guidance for profiling and optimizing Go applications with pprof, benchmarks, memory tuning, escape analysis, and concurrency patterns.
skill.summary: Analyze Go performance bottlenecks and apply measured optimization patterns.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium indicators, but review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, Go import blocks, and ordinary documentation text. The remaining low-risk indicators are legitimate Go toolchain commands and localhost pprof URLs used for performance profiling examples. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential access, persistence, or data exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Benign Go Toolchain Command Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown code fences as command execution. Manual review found actual shell examples only invoke standard Go profiling, build, and benchmark commands. These are legitimate for a Go performance skill, but users should run them only in trusted project directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown examples for go tool pprof, go build, and go test. They do not contain shell metacharacter injection, download commands, credential access, or destructive operations.

low_findings.title: Localhost Profiling Endpoint Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to localhost pprof endpoints for CPU, heap, and allocation profiling. This is expected for Go profiling documentation, but exposing pprof beyond localhost could leak runtime details in a real application.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs use http://localhost:6060 and target Go pprof paths. The surrounding text is profiling guidance, not outbound exfiltration or remote command control.

low_findings.title: Static Parser Misclassified Go and Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: Dynamic import, Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance findings were not confirmed. The cited content is Go import syntax, Markdown fences, performance examples, headings, or benchmark guidance rather than executable marketplace code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas shows documentation text and Go snippets only. No weak cryptographic algorithm, dynamic runtime import, reconnaissance routine, or Ruby execution context exists in the skill.

content.value_statement: Go services can waste CPU, memory, and latency when bottlenecks are guessed instead of measured. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide profiling, benchmarking, allocation reduction, and concurrency tuning.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to enable and inspect Go pprof CPU, heap, and allocation profiles.
Shows benchmark patterns with benchmem, benchtime, setup exclusion, and implementation comparison.
Recommends allocation reduction patterns such as slice preallocation, builders, and sync.Pool use.
Guides escape analysis review with Go compiler flags and heap allocation examples.
Covers concurrency tuning with worker pools, buffered channels, sharded locks, and sync.Map tradeoffs.
Highlights common hot path issues such as defer in loops, reflection-heavy JSON encoding, and interface boxing.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not an automated profiler or benchmark runner.
It does not replace production observability, load testing, or domain-specific latency analysis.
Some optimizations can reduce readability and should be justified by measurements.
The examples are general patterns and may need adaptation for specific Go versions and workloads.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Service Latency
use_cases.description: Use pprof and benchmarks to identify CPU-heavy functions, allocation hot spots, and expensive call paths before changing code.

use_cases.title: Reduce Memory Pressure
use_cases.description: Review heap profiles, escape analysis output, slice retention, pooling, and preallocation patterns to lower garbage collection cost.

use_cases.title: Review Performance PRs
use_cases.description: Check whether a proposed Go optimization has benchmarks, avoids common pitfalls, and preserves clarity where speed gains are small.

prompt_templates.title: Find Go Bottlenecks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-performance skill to help me profile this Go service. Start with pprof and benchmarks, then explain what data I should collect before changing code.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Allocations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-performance skill to review this Go function for avoidable allocations, retained backing arrays, escape analysis issues, and safer allocation reduction patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Concurrency
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-performance skill to evaluate this worker pool and channel design. Identify contention, blocking, buffering, and locking risks with benchmark ideas.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Performance Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-performance skill to review this Go performance PR. Compare benchmark quality, pprof evidence, memory behavior, concurrency risks, and readability tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Review a Go endpoint that allocates heavily under load.
output: A profiling plan covering CPU, heap, and allocation data.
Likely allocation sources to inspect, such as growing slices, string building, and interface conversions.
A prioritized set of measured changes with benchmark checks before and after each change.

output_examples.input: Assess whether a worker pool will improve throughput.
output: A concurrency review that checks worker count, channel buffering, result collection, and lock contention.
Benchmark scenarios for different input sizes and parallelism levels.
Warnings about changes that can increase latency or memory use despite higher throughput.

output_examples.input: Explain escape analysis output for a Go package.
output: A plain-language explanation of which values escape to the heap and why.
Suggestions for value returns, concrete types, or API changes where they preserve clarity.
A reminder to verify improvements with allocation benchmarks, not compiler output alone.

best_practices: Profile and benchmark before optimizing so the largest real bottleneck is addressed first.
Keep benchmarks representative by excluding setup time and reporting allocations when memory behavior matters.
Balance speed, readability, and maintenance cost when applying low-level Go optimizations.
anti_patterns: Do not optimize code paths that are not measured bottlenecks.
Do not expose pprof endpoints on public interfaces without access controls.
Do not replace clear code with complex pooling or locking unless benchmarks prove value.

faq.question: Does this skill run pprof or benchmarks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples that a user or agent can apply in a Go project.

faq.question: Can it help with production performance incidents?
faq.answer: Yes, it can guide profiling and triage, but production access and observability data remain the user responsibility.

faq.question: Is pprof safe to enable?
faq.answer: It is safest when bound to localhost or protected networks. Public pprof endpoints can expose runtime and workload details.

faq.question: Does it cover memory optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers allocation reduction, sync.Pool, slice retention, escape analysis, and benchmark validation.

faq.question: Does it support concurrency performance work?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers worker pools, buffered channels, lock contention, sharded caches, and sync.Map use cases.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill during code review?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompts support review of benchmarks, profiling evidence, hot paths, and performance tradeoffs.
